fields.description:
Wire **secrets (gitleaks) + High-Critical security (semgrep)** scanners as deterministic
ground truth feeding the hard auto-block (the LLM triages/prioritizes, never invents). The
integration shape below is settled by a derisk experiment (session_log a7e2-381c-6dc6-4d0c,
EXPERIMENT 1; tools: gitleaks 8.30.1, semgrep 1.168.0 against Engine B at
`src/rebar/grounding/`).

## Detector integration — what actually fits Engine B (experiment-grounded)

Engine B has exactly three backends (`registry.py:39-42`: `opengrep`, `ast-grep`, `metric`);
a detector is a native matcher YAML rule + `metadata.rebar_envelope`, parsed via `yaml.safe_load`.

- **semgrep = NATIVE fit.** semgrep is the opengrep rule format (opengrep is a semgrep fork);
  PROVEN that semgrep passes an arbitrary `metadata.rebar_envelope` through verbatim to its
  output and emits SARIF 2.1.0. So High-Critical security detectors ship as **detector YAML
  under `.rebar/detectors/` consumed by the existing `opengrep` backend** — no new backend.
  **Named rulesets (resolves the "ruleset unspecified" finding):** wrap the curated
  `p/secrets`-adjacent + `p/owasp-top-ten` + `p/cwe-top-25` rule subset, vendored as pinned
  rule YAML under `src/rebar/grounding/detectors/builtin/security_*.yaml` (NOT a live registry
  pull — reproducible/offline). Scanning is **diff-scoped** (changed files only) to bound
  per-patchset cost; full-repo is out of scope for v1.
- **gitleaks ≠ native fit.** PROVEN: gitleaks uses TOML config with rules compiled into the
  binary (no per-rule YAML to point `--config` at), so it cannot be an Engine B detector YAML.
  It DOES emit SARIF 2.1.0 (and JSON). So gitleaks is integrated via a **generic SARIF-ingest
  backend** (`BACKEND_SARIF`): a `_run_sarif()` runner that subprocess-invokes the tool
  (`gitleaks detect --report-format sarif`), then normalizes via
  `sarif.from_sarif(..., trust_rebar_bag=False)` to the evidence model. Chosen over a
  gitleaks-only 4th backend because semgrep ALSO emits SARIF, so one SARIF-ingest seam serves
  both secret-scanners and any future SARIF tool. (Decision recorded in the WS-ADR.)

## Fail-CLOSED lives in the CONSUMER, not the oracle (experiment-grounded)

PROVEN: Engine B's cardinal invariant is **fail-OPEN** — a missing/errored/timed-out binary
returns `abstain('no_tool'|'timeout'|'other')`, never blocks (`engine_b.py:346-359`;
`docs/grounding.md`). Forcing the oracle fail-closed would break every other consumer. So the
**fail-closed decision is made by THIS gate's Pass-3 consumer step**: an `abstain` on a
secrets/High-Critical-security detector is treated as **BLOCK + a coverage-gap annotation on
the blocking vote** (not silently passed, not an alternative to blocking). Advisory grounding
keeps the normal fail-open posture. Secrets/High-Critical criteria set `blocking_enabled=True`
via WS2's `threshold_for` resolver (the WS2→WS5 handoff: WS2 owns the resolver + the criterion
keys; WS5 sets those two criteria's `blocking_enabled=True` and owns the abstain→BLOCK mapping).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] semgrep High-Critical security detectors ship as vendored, pinned detector YAML under `src/rebar/grounding/detectors/builtin/security_*.yaml` (named ruleset subset documented), consumed by the existing `opengrep` backend; diff-scoped to changed files
- [ ] gitleaks integrated via a generic SARIF-ingest backend (`_run_sarif` → `sarif.from_sarif(trust_rebar_bag=False)`), subprocess-invoked with `--report-format sarif`; a test asserts a planted secret surfaces as a normalized evidence finding
- [ ] Secrets / High-Critical findings map to a hard BLOCK in Pass-3; everything else stays advisory at the starting (high) thresholds (`blocking_enabled=True` set for exactly those two criteria via WS2's resolver — explicit WS2→WS5 handoff)
- [ ] FAIL-CLOSED in the CONSUMER: RED-fixture test asserts an unavailable/errored secrets/security detector → the gate's Pass-3 step turns the `abstain` into BLOCK + a coverage-gap annotation on the vote, NEVER a silent pass; the oracle itself stays fail-open
- [ ] HAPPY-PATH test: a detector returning real findings surfaces them as ground-truth blocking findings
- [ ] An ADR records the SARIF-ingest-backend choice (vs a gitleaks-only 4th backend) and the consumer-side fail-closed boundary
